attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman is in active labour with her first child at 40 weeks gestation. She has severe immune thrombocytopenia with a platelet count of 40 x 10^9/L (150-400) which required intravenous immunoglobulin therapy during pregnancy. She has been pushing for two hours with minimal descent. The cardiotocograph shows reassuring fetal heart rate patterns.

On examination, she appears tired but well. Her pulse rate is 90/min, blood pressure 125/80 mmHg, respiratory rate 18/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 37.0°C. Vaginal examination reveals the fetal head at station +2, in the occipito-anterior position, with caput and moulding present.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Continue passive descent for one hour
attributes.explanation:
This scenario presents a primigravid woman with a prolonged second stage of labour and severe immune thrombocytopenia (ITP). The fetal head is at an appropriate station (+2) for instrumental delivery, and there are no signs of fetal compromise.

Forceps delivery is the most appropriate choice here. When properly applied, forceps provide controlled traction without increasing the risk of fetal intracranial haemorrhage. They are the preferred instrument when there are contraindications to vacuum extraction, such as in this case where maternal ITP puts the fetus at risk of thrombocytopenia. A pudendal nerve block should be considered for analgesia.

Vacuum-assisted delivery is contraindicated in this case due to the increased risk of fetal intracranial haemorrhage. Maternal ITP can cause fetal thrombocytopenia through transplacental passage of antiplatelet antibodies. The risk of subgaleal haemorrhage with vacuum extraction would be unacceptably high.

Immediate caesarean section would be overly aggressive at this stage, given that the fetus is not showing signs of compromise and forceps delivery is feasible. Caesarean section at this stage of labour carries increased risks of maternal morbidity and bleeding complications.

Continue passive descent for another hour would unnecessarily prolong the second stage of labour, which is already prolonged at two hours. This increases the risk of fetal compromise and maternal exhaustion.

Episiotomy alone is not an appropriate management strategy for prolonged second stage. While it may be performed as part of an instrumental delivery, it does not address the underlying issue of inadequate descent.

The key principles in managing prolonged second stage include assessing fetal position and station, evaluating for signs of obstruction or malposition, and choosing the appropriate method of assisted delivery when indicated. In cases of maternal ITP, careful consideration must be given to the risk of fetal bleeding complications.
learningPoints: Vacuum-assisted delivery is contraindicated in fetuses at risk of bleeding disorders including maternal ITP.
Forceps delivery is preferred over vacuum when there are contraindications to vacuum extraction.
Prolonged second stage of labour (>2 hours in nulliparous women) requires intervention if no descent is occurring.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Instrumental Vaginal Birth [Internet]. East Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2023 [cited 2024 Nov 17]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Instrumental-Vaginal-Birth.pdf
